sentences.text: A Desiderio.
Which is nothing.
Maybe a John.
Instead of an Oscar I'm going to get a John.
I certainly I'm going to get the--
There's nothing.
I'm just joking.
I'm going to get the John, anyway.
Well, before we got to Mauthausen, we went from Eisenerz up the mountain, and then we went down the mountain.
As we went down the mountain, the Germans started to shoot us, aimlessly.
Now some people were shot and he fell, and then the next fellow who went after it, maybe tripped or maybe stepped on it, or maybe could walk over it.

Now the people who were on the ground because they were shot-- there were quite a few, I guess it was about over 100-- the other people who were stumbling into it, they had fallen and since the rest of the people were intimidated by the shooting, they were trampling over it.
So there were many people who have been trampled to death by the thousands of Jews.
So not only the really hit people have died but also the ones who were stumbling.
Now, it went on for about-- I would say about 15, 20 minutes and then some German officers in one staff car, is whatever, arrived and then the shootings have stopped.
OK, now we went to a place called Steyr.
It's a small city, and as we walked through the main square, there was a young woman with a baby, a young kid in the carriage.
And he was dropping a bun in front of us.
I picked it up and was saving it with my friend, Suge, and then we split it in half.
And the German soldier who was watching us was running there and told the women, should I pick it up for you?
And she says, no, no, no, just leave it on the ground.
So and my friend went over to her and was asking for more.
Then the woman didn't dare to give us any more.
It was dropped deliberately, not for money just because she felt so different.
Now after this, we have arrived to Mauthausen.
We crossed over the Danube.
And it was in the evening, and the furnaces of the camp were blasting full force.
And we have seen the entrance, it was wrought iron.
And was written on it, it was 'arbeit macht frei' which means that work is going to free you.
Like heck, nobody was freed.
The Americans and the Russians were freeing you.
Now, there was no room inside of the camp, which I have visited just about two years ago.
And we were outside of the camp, and there was a tent, which covered a portion of their land outside of the Mauthausen concentration camp proper.
And we had been kept there for several days.
And I have to tell you an incident where we were carrying food.
Actually, it was just hot water with some noodles in a garbage can.
And we were carrying it, about four of us, and all of a sudden, another Jewish inmate was running full force and was tripping it, tripped the garbage can over.
The water was absorbed in the ground, and there were a few pieces of noodle, which was left.
And he picked it up with his spoon.
This was a most absurd thing to do, a Jew to another Jew.
But, of course, this Jew may have been in a camp for about two, three years, and that's the way he wanted to survive.
But it was still not right because about 15 or 20 of us didn't have anything to eat on that night.
So the following day when we went, we went 15 of us, because we were prepared that maybe somebody is going to do the same trick.
Now, this comes back, here I'm talking against Jews because Jews were coming, and they were pulling the blankets off the people who were sleeping.
And once you didn't have any blanket, you are going to die because it was in April, and April was still cold.
So that's why I told you I had even the boot inside of my sleeping bag.
Now, it came to the point where I didn't have my boot, which I was steal from Hungary.
It wasn't good enough anymore.
So my only choice was to get one, and the other one, I was taking a boot off a dead man.
And I just have taken it off, and I put it on.
These were the ones which were issued by the Germans.
It was a wooden sole and a canvas top, that one fitted everybody.
So from then on-- it was very close to the end of the war, and I knew that it's not going to be any more fashion show.
So I just-- he was the--
I had the Hungarian uniform to the bitter end, until the Americans burned it.
It didn't save me any more because we were in the concentration camp, and that was just nothing.
I had the proper wooden shoe, and we were walking.
Now, at that time, most of the-- now, I have to tell you, there is such a thing as a kapo, which was a Camp Polizei and there is a jupo, which one is Juden Polizei.
Now, in Mauthausen, in the camp, there was a man who was in charge, a Jewish man.
He was killed, I think, after when we were liberated, by the other Jews.
He had about four very well-fed companions who was keeping him from getting into trouble, like, that the rest of the Jews are not going to kill him because he was a very, very rude man.
He was just as rude as it would have been an SS.
He was a kapo, I guess, it's a Camp Polizei, Juden Polizei, whatever.
And we have-- that's a place where I have met my cousin, and my cousin was so-- he was about 42 years old and he was in such bad shape that he was asking me if I have any toothpaste.
I said, what toothpaste?
He says, well, I'm eating toothpaste, he said.
He was willing to eat anything which had some substance, which one was resembling food.
But he died about two or three days after.
He was in very, very bad shape.
How long were you at Mauthausen?
Oh, I wasn't there too long.
I think it must have been about 10 days.
I didn't do anything.
I was just trying to survive.
There was water at one point.
At this time, the water was running water because Mauthausen was built very properly by the Germans where there was water.
But there isn't much else.
We, as I told you, we were carrying food or soup or black something.
I was marching from [NON-ENGLISH] from Koszeg right up to Mauthausen, and from Mauthausen, we were walking to Gunskirchen.
Now Gunskirchen, we were walking in rain.
I think it must have been two solid days of rain, day and night.
We were in the water, but, again, my sleeping bag was waterproof.
And I was saved by this.
Now when we went to sleep, we were sleeping in such a manner that we were touching each other.
Like it was maybe 20 or 30 people who were lying side by side in order to keep warm.
Of course, you didn't keep warm, but it was still some kind of protection, except for the first and the last person.
Now in the evening, if you had to get up, then by the time you came back, you didn't have your spot anymore.
This was one of the first things what happened to day and night.
By that time, the people were so sick and tired of living that I remember there was one man who was sitting on a kind of a milestone.
It was a kilometer stone, but it didn't mean-- it was a stone.
And we were supposed to get up and go, and the German man said to him, now get up and go.
And he didn't even bother to answer.
So he said, if you don't get up and go, I am going to shoot you.
He didn't say a word.
He got out his revolver and shot him right there in the head.
We were so dehumanized, like, I and the rest of them.
So we didn't really mind what is going to happen to us or to anybody else.
We were absolutely senseless.
There was no impression on us.
Now, I-- before we got to Gunskirchen, a day before, there was a stop.
Well, this friend of mine-- it wasn't really a friend of mine, but with whom I was weathering the winter part of the concentration camp-- somehow or other collapsed.
There was a German man-- as we were walking.
The German soldier asked me, very politely, soll ich schiesen, which means, should I kill him?
Soll ich schiesen.
Schiesen is to shoot.
And I said, no, no, no, and I was really adamant about it.
And I thought, well, for heaven sakes.
What the heck are you doing?
He says, let me die.
I cannot walk anymore.
I said, sure you can walk.
You see, just across the city we are going through, the people are already at rest?
So I grabbed him, and I put his arm over my neck.
And I lifted him by my right hand under his arm, and I said, now let's get going.
And he was just dragging himself, and was about a kilometer or something and maybe a little bit longer.
And when we arrived, then I told him, see, now we are here.
And then he says, OK and what now?
I said, now you go, and we go to sleep.
And in the morning everything is going to be all right.
Sure thing, the following morning everything was all right.
Two weeks after liberation he dropped dead.
So that my whole effort was absolutely nothing.
All what I did was prolonged his suffering.
So so much for humor.
Now then we got into Gunskirchen, and Gunskirchen, it was a freshly built concentration-- built barracks.
And the war was over in about 10 days time.
And I was just surprised that the Germans had the ambition to build a brand new camp for Jews, but they did it anyway.
Now there we had this idiotic SS people who were just shooting into barracks.
And if somebody was shot, he was yelling, I got shot, and then dropped dead.
Then he ordered somebody to pick him up and take him out and bury.
Once I was so lucky that somebody who has died in our barrack, and he told me, now you pick him up and bury him.
So we had those wheelbarrows, and I had to put the man into a wheelbarrow.
And I was supposed to take him some distance from the camp, not a long distance, and I was digging a hole, a very shallow hole.
And I put the man in, and I covered it with some dirt.
I mean, I couldn't dig a six feet grave anymore.
I was just too tired.
That was the time when I started to feel very, very tired.
As I was walking, I was walking in a kind of a daze.
I knew where I'm going to walk, but I didn't know that I am going to make it.
Before or during?
We were liberated, I was liberated on May the 4th.
I think it must have been 10 days or so.

About the liberation, now all of a sudden we saw that all the guards of the camp-- except the SS, SS were running away about a day or two before the end of the war, and they were throwing grenade into his gun, or whatever he had.
And so blow it up and he was changing clothing and disappeared.
But the rest of them were gathering in the middle of the camp and very nicely attired were sitting there and were waiting for the Americans to come.
The Americans came, and they're gone.
And we started to eat very solid meat, conserved, how do you-- canned meat.
And they started to die because of diarrhea, which was a result of eating that.
Now, after this the Americans have kept us and put us on trucks.
About two days later, we were transported into a barrack, which was used by the Germans for the soldiers.
And we were put there in beds, and most of us had diarrhea.
But before, I have to commemorate a very, very good hearted, reasonable person whose name I don't know.
I saw him giving bread to people who couldn't move anymore.
It was after the liberation.
Another Jew had a loaf of bread and cut small pieces of bread and was feeding it to other Jews who couldn't move anymore.
Now I will never, ever forget it.
How much sacrifice he made because it wasn't his business to keep somebody else alive, and he was trying his level best.
I was too young to understand at that time and to imitate it, but it was something special, which is really remarkable.
So then, we were given over to the Germans in that barracks.
And the Germans were supposed to look after us.
The Americans said to the Germans, see German, these are Jews you wanted to kill, now you have to feed them.
And I have seen that the Germans were putting water into the milk and it became blue because it was so little milk in it.
And then most everything was stolen, and we weren't much better off during the American-- after the American liberation than before.
The only thing is that we weren't shot dead by the Americans.
How long did it take before you got to Hungary?
Well, it has taken quite a long time because I was in that hospital for a while.
And then later on, we had to go out and back for beg from Austrians for food because the food which we have received from the Americans wasn't satisfactory.
They gave us dried peas, and it was mushy.
And we were sick.
We were not ready to eat dried-- and so we went out, and we were begging for food.
And we were getting some soup and some bread and something which we could eat.
Well, eventually, after about three weeks, four weeks, we were put on a train.
And interestingly, they didn't leave us anything to eat.
The Americans put us on the train without any food.
So I don't know what did they think.
There was no dining car.
There were only cattle wagons.
And, of course, we have weren't 80 to a cattle wagon.
We were just side by side, as many Jews as they could accommodate.
Still, in a sleeping bag.

So we arrived to-- no, no, no, sorry, before we got to the train, we were-- no, the sleeping bag wasn't there anymore because as we had been liberated and we were taken to that barracks, to those barracks, before we went in there, they were getting rid of the lice.
So whatever I had, it was burnt.
I remember I had a royal blue turtleneck sweater, which I carried for 20 months because I thought, the situation is not good, but it could be worse.
So I was saving it.
It was brand new from home, and the Americans were burning it eventually.
Now we were DDT'd, which means that we had to be naked and lift up our arms and so underneath our-- the lice underneath our arm, in the armpits could be killed.
And we were wiped all over front and back and then we were given some kind of clothing.
No shirt, just pants and a kind of a jacket.
But there was no undershirt.
There was no shirt, or nothing.
That's the way we were, and then we got into the-- they made a hospital out of this edifice.
And then we went, and we got on the train.
And the train carried us right to the Russian border.
Like, it was a Russian sector of Austria.
It wasn't the Russian border.
It was a communist section of Austria.
And then we were walking there toward the Hungarian border.
And one evening we were put up in a loft of a farmer.
We were sleeping there in straw.
And the following day we started to walk again, and we got something to eat for free.
On the other hand, when we arrived to Hungary we got nothing to eat.
Hungarian peasant wanted to have something.
What do you give me?
I said, well, what do I have?
I have nothing.
I said, why don't I give you nothing?
Even on the train station, they were selling water, regular water, drinking water for money or for to barter to give something.
I said, what can I do?
I have nothing.
I just came from a concentration camp.
He says, then we don't give you water.
So that was the attitude of the Hungarians.
Now the way I got to Hungary is we were asking a Russian truck driver to let us into the truck.
So he stopped for us.
We got in, and then he didn't ask any questions.
He was taking us to the nearest Hungarian village, and then he left the truck.
And there we were.
We saw that he doesn't care about any more with us, so we got out.
And we were looking for the Hungarian authorities who said, there is an office for Jews.
So then he went there, and we were telling our story.
And they said, well, here's something to eat, and then get on the train.
So we got on the train.
And I had this paper from Mauthausen that we were liberating, which one I have shown you.
Liberated-- and we have shown to the conductor.
And the conductor stamped it on the back, and we got back to Budapest.
In Budapest, we have taken the streetcar.
I still had some money, of the money of which one we got about a year before from the Gentile woman.

I have to tell you before we finish, when the Gentile woman's husband came back, he kept the money, and he didn't give me back the money what the Gentile woman, his wife, was willing to give it to me.
The what?
Well, I was arriving by streetcar to the middle of the city where our apartment building was, where we were living with my parents.
And my schoolmate Sammy had asked me, what are you doing here?
I said, what are you doing here?
So the two of us survived, and he said, I'm going to take you home, to his home.
And my fiance was living not very far from him.
And he said, well, I'm going to give you a shirt because without a shirt, how can you present yourself?
I said, easily.
I'll just walk in.

He gave me-- You know, I was so tired, so weak, that while we were waiting for the streetcar to come, I was sitting down on the curb because I just didn't have any strength to wait standing up for the streetcar.
When I was shaving myself with those safety shaver, this bone on both sides, was sticking out so badly that for a month when I was shaving myself I cut out the skin because it was in the way.

I mean, I don't think I was weighing more than, I would say, about 65, 70 pounds, I was-- all my bones were sticking out, and it has taken me months and months and months before-- You know, on my honeymoon, I had diarrhea, and I couldn't make it to the toilet.
Would you like to have a honeymoon like this?
How long did you stay in Hungary?
Well, we were staying there until 1949, April 17, when we have decided that this is not for us.
And we were going to Austria through Czechoslovakia, and we had a smuggler who was surprised, who came up to our apartment and said, well, this is a very nice apartment.
I don't have an apartment like this.
And why do you want to go?

I said because we have an apartment like this, and we are not trusted by the communists because we are-- you know, the Hungarian leader, Rakosi Matyas, said we like the former fascist Hungarians better than the Jews who are winking toward the West.
He was a Jewish man.
He was the head of the Communist party, and later he became the leader of Hungary.
He became, I would say, the president of Hungary.
He was a person who was in charge.
But he got his-- in 1952, so I understand, he was taken to Moscow, and he became a no person.
From Austria, we went to Salzburg where we were in a kind of a camp.

And then eventually, we had some-- my father sent us some money to Switzerland, and I got part of the-- the Swiss were taking, the men who was dealing with the Swiss authorities, has taken one third of the money what my father sent out, and the rest we have received.
And what was the question?
Well, we came to Canada in June, June 13, I guess, in Halifax.
And from Halifax, we got by train to Toronto.
In those days, there was no such a thing that you arrive into Canada and you are going to be looked after by the government.
Our relatives had to send the money, pay in the money for the train ride from Halifax to Toronto, and that's the way we have arrived.
